explanation:
The requirement described in the natural language statement is that in PCV or PSV modes, if the GUI sets an inspiratory pause (indicated by 'inspiratoryPauseButton'), the system must: (1) wait until the current inspiration phase ends; (2) at the end of inspiration (i.e., when 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd' occurs), if the pause is still requested, both inspiratory and expiratory valves ('inValveClose' and 'outValveClose') must remain closed, and this continues until the GUI stops the pause ('!inspiratoryPauseButton').

The LTL formula begins by globally ('G') considering when either PCV or PSV modes are active ('PCVMode | PSVMode'). If an inspiratory pause is requested ('inspiratoryPauseButton'), then, at the next end of an inspiratory phase ('F inspiratoryPhaseEnd'), if the pause is still required ('inspiratoryPauseButton'), then from that point and as long as 'inspiratoryPauseButton' holds, both valves should be closed ('inValveClose & outValveClose'), and this holds until 'inspiratoryPauseButton' becomes false (user cancels pause).

The formula captures the requirement step by step:
- In PCV/PSV, if inspiratory pause is requested,
- Wait until the end of the next inspiration ('F inspiratoryPhaseEnd'),
- If pause is still requested, close both valves,
- Keep valves closed until pause is no longer requested.

No previously given LTL property addressed this traceability or progression from pause request to end of inspiration to pause activation, and all steps are mapped only using atomic propositions as required.
output_LTL: G((PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton -> F (inspiratoryPhaseEnd & inspiratoryPauseButton & X ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U !inspiratoryPauseButton)))

explanation:
The requirement states that in PCV or PSV modes, when the inspiratory pause is set by the GUI, the ventilator should, after the next inspiration phase ends, if the requirement for pause still exists, close both the inspiratory and expiratory valves until the pause is stopped by the GUI. In atomic propositions, 'PCVMode' and 'PSVMode' signal being in either of these modes. 'inspiratoryPauseButton' indicates the pause is requested by the GUI, while 'inspiratoryPause' indicates the pause is in effect. 'inValveClose' and 'outValveClose' indicate the respective valves are closed. The property must state: (i) If in PCV or PSV mode AND user sets inspiratory pause, then after the next inspiratory phase ends, and as long as the pause is still requested, both valves remain closed UNTIL the pause is stopped. This is captured as: whenever (PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton holds, after the next inspiratoryPhaseEnd, globally for as long as inspiratoryPauseButton continues to hold, both inValveClose and outValveClose must hold, until inspiratoryPauseButton no longer holds.

Older LTLs might miss: (a) correct scoping to 'after the next inspiration phase ends', (b) ensuring both valves stay closed for the full duration the pause is requested, (c) tie to only PCV/PSV modes, and (d) correct UNLV release upon pause being stopped.

This LTL attempts to correctly capture the transient trigger, scope, and required duration using combinations of 'X' (next) and 'U' (until).
output_LTL: G((PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton -> F(inspiratoryPhaseEnd & X((inspiratoryPauseButton) U (!inspiratoryPauseButton & G((inValveClose & outValveClose) U !inspiratoryPauseButton)))) )

explanation:
This LTL property formalizes the requirement that in PCV or PSV modes, when the Inspiratory Pause button is activated by the GUI (represented by 'inspiratoryPauseButton'), after the next inspiratory phase ends ('inspiratoryPhaseEnd'), if the pause is still required (modeled by 'inspiratoryPause' remaining true), the inspiratory and expiratory valves must both be closed ('inValveClose' and 'outValveClose') and must remain closed until the pause is stopped (i.e., 'inspiratoryPause' becomes false). It ensures the logic is responsive to the user, sensitive to the phase transitions, and maintains the correct valve states as described in the requirement.

Why previous draft LTL properties may be insufficient: Without ensuring synchrony over the exact sequence—button press, waiting for inspiration to end, and then closing valves as long as pause required—some properties might either trigger the pause at the wrong moment, forget to keep the valves closed, or not reset correctly when the pause is stopped. This formula explicitly captures all these behavioral nuances.
output_LTL: G ( ( (PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton ) -> ( X ( ( !inspiratoryPhase | X inspiratoryPhaseEnd ) U ( inspiratoryPhaseEnd & ( inspiratoryPause U ( (!inspiratoryPause) | ( (inValveClose & outValveClose) U !inspiratoryPause ) ) ) ) ) ) )

explanation:
The input describes the following requirement: When (in PCV or PSV modes) the 'Inspiratory Pause' is set by the GUI, after the next inspiration phase ends, if 'Inspiratory Pause' is still required, then both inspiratory and expiratory valves close until 'Inspiratory Pause' is stopped by the GUI. 

In atomic proposition terms: 'PCVMode' or 'PSVMode' (modes active); 'inspiratoryPauseButton' (pause set via GUI); 'inspiratoryPause' (the Inspiratory Pause is active); 'inValveClose' and 'outValveClose' (valves closed).

The LTL property should capture that whenever in PCV or PSV and after inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed (indicating a request), then after the next end of inspiration (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), if the request ('inspiratoryPauseButton') is still active, an inspiratoryPause (and thus both valves closed) must be maintained until the request is cancelled.

We can't use numerical comparison or memory in LTL, so we'll focus on a property that: Whenever (PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton holds, then after an inspiratoryPhaseEnd, whenever inspiratoryPauseButton still holds, then globally until inspiratoryPauseButton is false, both valves remain closed (inspiratoryPause-> inValveClose & outValveClose).

The previous (if any) LTL translations may fail to properly capture the temporal modality (trigger after next inspiration end) or might fail to specify both valves should stay closed as long as the pause is requested. This property attempts to precisely encode the described causal chaining.

Property:
Globally, if in PCV or PSV and inspiratoryPauseButton becomes true, then after next inspiratoryPhaseEnd, if inspiratoryPauseButton is still true, globally until inspiratoryPauseButton goes false, inspiratoryPause implies inValveClose and outValveClose.

The goal is to encode that in PCV or PSV mode, if the inspiratory pause is requested by the GUI (inspiratoryPauseButton), then after the next inspiratory phase ends (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), as long as inspiratoryPause remains required, both the inspiratory and expiratory valves are kept closed. The closing of the valves can be represented by inValveClose and outValveClose (both are atomic propositions). The pause ends when the pause button is no longer set (i.e., !inspiratoryPauseButton), but the system may also independently signal the end of the pause with !inspiratoryPause. This property assumes the inspiratoryPause atomic proposition is active while the pause is ongoing. The formula ensures that, globally, whenever in either PCV or PSV mode and the pause is requested, after the next end of inspiration, if inspiratoryPause is still active, both valves are closed as long as inspiratoryPause holds.
output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton) -> (F(inspiratoryPhaseEnd & inspiratoryPause) -> ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U !inspiratoryPause)))

explanation:
The natural language requirement describes the expected behavior in PCV or PSV ventilation modes when the user (via GUI) sets the Inspiratory Pause. The behavior is: if Inspiratory Pause is set, then after the current inspiration phase finishes (at 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd'), if the Inspiratory Pause is still set, both the inspiratory and expiratory valves close, and remain closed until the Inspiratory Pause is stopped.

Using the available atomic propositions, we capture the triggering and logic as follows:
- 'PCVMode | PSVMode' is true if either PCV mode or PSV mode is active.
- 'inspiratoryPauseButton' is true when the Inspiratory Pause is set by GUI. We must wait for the end of the current inspiration (i.e., when 'inspiratoryPauseButton' first becomes true, we look for the next 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd').
- After 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd', if Inspiratory Pause is still required (i.e., 'inspiratoryPauseButton' is still true), both valves should close and stay closed (i.e., 'inValveClose & outValveClose') until 'inspiratoryPauseButton' becomes false.

The LTL property below expresses: Whenever we are in PSV or PCV mode and the Inspiratory Pause is requested (i.e., 'inspiratoryPauseButton' becomes true), then, after the next 'inspiratoryPhaseEnd', if Inspiratory Pause is still requested, both valves are closed ('inValveClose & outValveClose') and remain so until Inspiratory Pause is no longer set ('!inspiratoryPauseButton').

Previous and incorrect properties might conflate the exact sequencing (e.g., closing valves immediately at 'inspiratoryPauseButton' without waiting for the end of inspiration phase), or fail to maintain valve closure until the Inspiratory Pause is cleared.

The translation faithfully represents the described behavior using the available atomic propositions and LTL operators.
output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton & X((!inspiratoryPhaseEnd) U inspiratoryPhaseEnd) & X((!inspiratoryPhaseEnd) U inspiratoryPhaseEnd)) -> X((inspiratoryPauseButton) -> (inValveClose & outValveClose) U (!inspiratoryPauseButton)))

explanation:
The input requirement states that in PCV or PSV modes, if the inspiratory pause is set by the GUI (i.e., inspiratoryPauseButton becomes true), then after the next inspiration phase ends (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), if the pause is still required, both the inspiratory and expiratory valves must remain closed (represented by !inValveOpen & !outValveOpen) until the pause is stopped by the GUI (¬inspiratoryPauseButton). This property captures this as follows: whenever we are in PCVMode or PSVMode, and inspiratoryPauseButton gets set, then after the next inspiratoryPhaseEnd occurs, as long as inspiratoryPauseButton remains true, both valves are held closed, until inspiratoryPauseButton becomes false. The use of the 'until' operator helps capture the 'until the pause is stopped' part, and the sequence of events is encoded via triggers and 'until'.

An incorrect or incomplete LTL might miss (a) the dependency on the next inspiratoryPhaseEnd after the pause button is set, (b) the requirement to maintain the closed valves state only as long as the inspiratoryPauseButton remains true, or (c) restrict the state to only one mode. This translation accounts for all these by properly sequencing the detection of the pause button with the phase end, and enforcing valve closure for the required duration.
output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & X(inspiratoryPauseButton)) -> F((inspiratoryPhaseEnd & inspiratoryPauseButton) & ((!(inValveOpen) & !(outValveOpen)) U (!inspiratoryPauseButton))))

explanation: The requirement states: in PCV or PSV mode (i.e., PCVMode or PSVMode active), if the inspiratoryPauseButton is set (user requests inspiratory pause), then after the end of the next inspiration phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), both the inspiratory and expiratory valves should close (inValveClose and outValveClose) and remain closed as long as the inspiratoryPauseButton remains set. When inspiratoryPauseButton is no longer set (user stops inspiratory pause), the valves can re-open. This temporal ordering can be encoded as: whenever in PCV or PSV mode and the inspiratoryPauseButton becomes set, then after the next inspiratoryPhaseEnd, if the button is still set, both valves will be closed and remain so until inspiratoryPauseButton is cleared. This LTL captures this with: globally, if we're in PCV or PSV mode and the button is set, then whenever the next inspiratoryPhaseEnd happens and the button is still set, from that point on, the valves will be closed until the button is cleared. Other previously given LTLs may fail to enforce the mutual closure of both valves, may not appropriately tie the closure to both the next inspiratoryPhaseEnd and the actual ongoing state of the button, or may prematurely trigger or release the pause before explicit user clearance.
output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton) -> F(inspiratoryPhaseEnd & inspiratoryPauseButton & X((inValveClose & outValveClose) U !inspiratoryPauseButton)))

explanation: This formula ensures that in either PCV or PSV mode, when the inspiratoryPauseButton is pressed, the system waits until the end of the next inspiration phase (inspiratoryPhaseEnd). After inspiratoryPhaseEnd, as long as the inspiratoryPauseButton remains active, inspiratoryPause, inValveClose, and outValveClose are all held active. Once the button is released, these conditions must cease. This property properly brackets the waiting period 'until' inspiratoryPhaseEnd, triggers the pause actions, and removes those actions upon the GUI removing the request. The parentheses are balanced and the logic matches the requirement: (1) detect mode & button press, (2) delay until next insp-phase-end, (3) enter pause as long as button held, (4) end pause on button release. It avoids all numerical/comparison forms and uses only LTL-allowed constructs. Previous attempts failed due to misplaced parentheses at the end; this version uses a correct, explicit until-then-finally-while structure.
output_LTL: G( ((PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton & X((!inspiratoryPhaseEnd) U inspiratoryPhaseEnd)) -> ( (inspiratoryPauseButton U (!inspiratoryPauseButton)) & G( inspiratoryPauseButton -> (inspiratoryPause & inValveClose & outValveClose) ) ) )

explanation:
The natural language statement describes expected ventilator behavior in PCV or PSV modes when an inspiratory pause is requested by the user via the GUI. Specifically: If (a) the ventilator is in PCVMode or PSVMode, (b) the inspiratoryPauseButton is set (requested), then after the very next inspiration phase ends (inspiratoryPhaseEnd), if the GUI has still not cancelled the pause (so inspiratoryPauseButton remains set), BOTH the inspiratory and expiratory valves should close (inValveClose & outValveClose), and they should stay closed UNTIL the GUI cancels the pause (inspiratoryPauseButton is released). 

This LTL encodes that: Globally, whenever we are in PCV or PSV mode and the inspiratory pause is requested, after the next end of inspiration there will be a moment (in the next state) where (if the pause is still requested) both valves are closed and will stay closed as long as the pause is held. (Note: the property does not cover explicitly how the pause is started and stopped, since atomic props are not available for pause end, but as per description, we model unbroken 'inspiratoryPauseButton' holding.)

Previous LTLs may not capture the precise sequencing: that the pause can only begin after the next inspiration phase ends (not immediately after requesting), and that both valves must be closed through the duration of the pause, and must not re-open prematurely if the pause is still required. This version precisely encodes the temporal waiting and holding behavior.
output_LTL: G(((PCVMode | PSVMode) & inspiratoryPauseButton) -> X((!inspiratoryPhase) U (inspiratoryPhaseEnd & ((inspiratoryPauseButton) -> ((inValveClose & outValveClose) U (!inspiratoryPauseButton))))))
